prompt: I'm prepping for a warm intro to Meridian Financial Group -- the CRO Sarah Chen just got replaced about two months ago by a guy named Marcus Webb. He came from Stripe I think, or maybe one of the other payments companies. Meridian is a mid-market fintech doing B2B payments processing, they've been around maybe 8-9 years and I know they're north of $100M ARR because their CEO mentioned it on a podcast last year. We sell revenue intelligence software -- conversation analytics, deal inspection, forecast accuracy, that kind of thing. New CRO at a company that size almost always means the board wanted better pipeline visibility and forecast discipline. Our champion at Meridian was on Sarah's team and I'm worried he's going to get reorged out. Can you do the full research workup on Marcus Webb and the current state of Meridian so I know what I'm walking into? I need to figure out if our existing relationship survives this transition or if we're starting from scratch.
expectations: To protect an existing deal relationship at risk of reorg, the research brief identifies the CRO transition as the central strategic factor — WHO IS MARCUS WEBB includes verified career background (confirming or correcting the Stripe attribution with source), tenure calculated as approximately 2 months with signal interpretation that a new CRO at <6 months is a HOT receptivity window per the skill's signal criteria

To give the AE competitive intelligence on the account before the warm intro, WHAT IS MERIDIAN FINANCIAL GROUP covers real business model (B2B payments processing), revenue estimate sourced to the CEO podcast mention plus any corroborating data, headcount with trend direction, and market position — the company is NOT conflated with any other entity named Meridian

To identify whether this is a 'defend the deal' or 'expand the deal' situation, signal classification categorizes the CRO replacement as a HOT signal with source and date, and separately evaluates supporting signals — job postings for revenue operations or sales operations roles, reorg indicators, or changes in go-to-market strategy that reveal whether Marcus Webb is building a new team or inheriting the existing one

To arm the AE with a narrative that resonates with a new CRO's first-100-days priorities, the pain assessment constructs a specific causal chain: CRO replacement implies board-level dissatisfaction with revenue execution, which creates immediate demand for pipeline visibility, forecast accuracy, and deal inspection — connecting Marcus Webb's likely priorities directly to the revenue intelligence product category

To make the warm intro feel informed rather than generic, at least one hook is identified that is specific to Marcus Webb or the transition event — a post Marcus made about revenue methodology, an initiative he led at Stripe, or a Meridian job posting that signals his new priorities — not a generic 'new CROs need visibility' hook

To determine the right approach given the champion-at-risk situation, the recommended first touch addresses whether to lead through the existing champion or go direct to Marcus Webb — specifying channel and tone appropriate for a new C-level executive in his first 90 days, and flagging the reorg risk as a factor that affects timing and approach
